primary_diagnosis.name: Mild acrofacial vitiligo with secondary acute health anxiety

comorbidities.name: Acute health anxiety (secondary to the skin finding)

pathophysiology_summary: Vitiligo is an acquired autoimmune depigmenting disorder in which CD8+ T-cell-mediated destruction of epidermal melanocytes produces sharply demarcated, chalk-white (fully depigmented) macules. The acrofacial pattern favours periorificial facial skin (perioral, periocular) and the distal digits, matching this presentation (oral commissure plus fingertip). The skin architecture is otherwise normal — no scale, induration, atrophy or barrier disruption — which is what distinguishes it from infective, chemical and ischaemic mimics. The lesion is chronic and stable; the 'sudden onset' is a discovery event, not true acute evolution. The peri-lesional erythema is reactive, caused by the patient's own vigorous mechanical scrubbing, not inflammation or infection. The dominant acute problem is psychological: catastrophic misinterpretation of a benign cosmetic finding driving disproportionate distress and an urge to seek emergency care.

differential.evidence_basis: Chalk-white, sharply demarcated macule in a periorificial facial site — classic depigmentation.
A common cause of pale patches; must be excluded by the presence/absence of fine powdery scale and by the depigmented (not merely hypopigmented) quality.
Depigmentation can follow contact with phenolic/bleaching agents; excluded by a negative exposure history.
Feared by the patient; excluded by intact painless skin with normal perfusion and no ulceration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should hold the benign depigmentation as leading while explicitly keeping the treatable/serious mimics (tinea, chemical, ischaemia) on the list — NOT anchor on the patient's feared 'flesh-eating infection'.

differential.evidence_basis: Painless, non-scaly, chalk-white macule with a second matching fingertip lesion (acrofacial distribution) and no chemical exposure.
Made less likely by the absence of scale and the fully depigmented (not hypopigmented) quality, but cannot be excluded with certainty without in-person examination.
Negative exposure history and typical autoimmune acrofacial distribution.
Painless, intact, well-perfused skin with no ulceration — no features of tissue death.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The painless, non-scaly, chalk-white character plus a second acrofacial lesion and a negative chemical history should collapse the serious mimics; residual uncertainty about fine scale (not judgeable on video) is the reason to recommend in-person confirmation, not the reason to escalate acutely.

differential.diagnosis: Mild acrofacial vitiligo with secondary health anxiety
differential.evidence_basis: Benign, stable, painless depigmentation in a classic distribution; the acute clinical need is de-escalation of distress and prevention of an unnecessary emergency presentation, with routine confirmatory review.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A correct final position is a presumptive benign diagnosis with appropriate telehealth humility (cannot fully examine) and a routine in-person referral — NOT emergency escalation.

red_flags.description: Ulceration, breakdown or bleeding of the patch
red_flags.clinical_significance: Intact skin argues strongly against necrosis, aggressive infection or malignancy; its presence would change the picture entirely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuinely ulcerating or necrotic lesion mistaken for benign vitiligo would delay urgent care.

red_flags.description: Heat, severe pain or rapidly spreading rash with fever (features of aggressive infection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Painless, afebrile, non-spreading lesion excludes cellulitis, necrotising infection and systemic sepsis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing a spreading soft-tissue infection with systemic features could be life-threatening.

red_flags.description: Thick, powdery or crusty scale, or rapid centrifugal spread of pale patches over days
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise a superficial fungal infection (pityriasis versicolor / tinea) that is treatable and warrants reassessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated spreading dermatophyte/malassezia infection mislabelled as vitiligo would persist and spread.

red_flags.description: Escalating psychological distress with inability to cope, or thoughts of self-harm
red_flags.clinical_significance: The acute problem is disproportionate health anxiety; severe or self-harm-associated distress would itself warrant urgent mental-health support.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overlooking overwhelming distress risks an acute mental-health crisis and an avoidable ED presentation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Framing / visceral bias under patient panic
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept up by the patient's terror of a 'flesh-eating fungus' and over-triaging a benign, painless, stable macule to the emergency department.
Accepting the patient's infective/burn framing and pursuing it rather than reading the reassuring painless, non-scaly, chalk-white features.
Interpreting the peri-lesional redness — caused by the patient's own scrubbing — as inflammation or cellulitis.
Treating a lesion discovered tonight as truly acute; it is a chronic, previously-obscured finding, which changes urgency.

Declaring it definitely benign when fine scale and texture cannot be judged on video — a superficial fungal mimic still needs in-person exclusion, so the honest action is routine confirmatory review, not either extreme.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Presence or absence of fine powdery scale on palpation/scraping
Skin induration, atrophy or subtle textural change on palpation
Capillary refill and perfusion of the surrounding tissue by examination
Skin scraping / mycology to definitively exclude pityriasis versicolor
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: De-escalate the acute distress, give clear reassurance about the benign features that ARE ascertainable, explicitly acknowledge that a superficial fungal or chemical mimic cannot be fully excluded remotely, and arrange a routine (not emergency) in-person GP or dermatology review to confirm the diagnosis and discuss treatment.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is NOT under-diagnosis of an emergency (there is none) but two opposite errors: (1) over-triage to ED under the patient's panic, and (2) over-confident dismissal that fails to arrange in-person confirmation of a fungal/chemical mimic.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Vitiligo is a benign autoimmune depigmentation; management includes photoprotection of depigmented skin, camouflage, and topical calcineurin inhibitors or corticosteroids for repigmentation, plus screening for associated autoimmune disease.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australasian College of Dermatologists — Vitiligo patient information
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Depigmented skin lacks natural sun protection and must be protected with sunscreen; treatment is elective and best discussed in a routine dermatology setting.
